title: Lesson: The Quarter Note
text:
Checking sign-in…

  
    
    
    Sign in
    Create
    Google
  

  

  
    
    Sign out
The quarter note gets one beat in common time. In a 4/4 measure you can fit four of them — one per beat. We count them simply: 1 – 2 – 3 – 4.

title: Lesson: The Quarter Note
text:
Hear it, then say it
Before reading a single quarter note, you should be able to audiate a steady beat — feel it internally — and chant a neutral syllable on each beat. Only after the sound is secure do we attach the symbol.

title: Lesson: The Quarter Note
text:
Practice steps

Tap a steady beat with your foot.
Chant “bah” on each tap for four beats.
Now look at four quarter notes and chant them while tapping.

When you’re ready, head to Practice → Rhythm Patterns and play a few quarter-note patterns. Your time on this page and your plays over there are both recorded automatically.






Tip



This page is being timed. Stay as long as you need; switch tabs and the timer pauses. Come back and it resumes.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text:
Checking sign-in…

  
    
    
    Sign in
    Create
    Google
  

  

  
    
    Sign out
Tonal patterns are short groups of pitches we learn by ear first, with solfège, before we ever name a key signature. The foundation is the major tonic and dominant.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text:
Stepwise motion
The most basic tonal pattern is stepwise: Do – Re – Mi – Fa – Sol and back down. Sing it on a neutral syllable first, then with solfège, then connect it to what you see on the staff.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text:
Tonic and dominant

Tonic (Do – Mi – Sol): the “home” chord, restful.
Dominant (Sol – Ti – Re): the “tension” chord that wants to resolve home.

Hearing the difference between these two is the single most important tonal skill in beginning band.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text:
Practice steps

Sing Do, then find Sol.
Sing the tonic arpeggio, then the dominant.
Go to Practice → Tonal Patterns and play major stepwise patterns, singing along with solfège.

title: Music Practice (demo)
text:
Checking sign-in…

  
    
    
    Sign in
    Create
    Google
  

  

  
    
    Sign out
This is a throwaway Quarto site wired to Supabase so you can get a feel for the new concepts before integrating any of it into your real site:

title: Music Practice (demo)
text:
Try this

Make sure you’ve run the one-time setup (see README.md): create the Supabase tables and paste your URL + anon key into config.js.
Use the bar at the top to create an account and sign in.
Wander through a few Lessons — linger on each, switch browser tabs and come back (watch that hidden time isn’t counted).
Open a Practice page, press play, click some notes.
Open the Teacher Report to see your time and actions per page.







Note



For the first pass, sign in with email/password — it needs no extra setup. Google sign-in also works but requires a Google Cloud OAuth client and a redirect URL; see README.md.

title: Music Practice (demo)
section: What’s new here vs. a plain Quarto site
text:
What’s new here vs. a plain Quarto site
Nothing about your authoring changes — these are ordinary .qmd pages. The only additions live in _quarto.yml (three include-* lines) and three small files (config.js, track.js, and the _includes/ partials). That’s the whole integration surface.

title: Lesson: Dotted Rhythms
text:
Checking sign-in…

  
    
    
    Sign in
    Create
    Google
  

  

  
    
    Sign out
A dot adds half the value of the note again. A dotted half note (normally two beats) becomes three beats. A dotted quarter becomes one-and-a-half.

title: Lesson: Dotted Rhythms
text:
The dotted-quarter-plus-eighth figure
The classic figure is a dotted quarter followed by an eighth — the “long- short” pattern you hear at the start of countless tunes. It’s tricky because the eighth note lands on the and of beat two, not on a beat.

title: Lesson: Dotted Rhythms
text:
Practice steps

Chant a steady “1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &”.
Hold the dotted quarter across “1 & 2”, then say the eighth on “&”.
Try it slowly, then bring it up to tempo.

Then go to Practice → Tunes and find a tune that uses this figure. Play it a few times and listen for the long-short.






Tip



Notice this lesson tracks separately from the quarter-note lesson — the report will show your time on each page, not just “lessons” as a lump.

title: Accompaniment Styles
text:
Checking sign-in…

  
    
    
    Sign in
    Create
    Google
  

  

  
    
    Sign out
  












  
    These patterns are built into the accompaniment engine and can be selected on any tune page.
    Each style adapts automatically to the tune's meter and chord chart.
    Styles marked any meter work in 2/4, 3/4, 4/4, and 6/8; fixed-meter styles
    fall back to block chords when the meter doesn't match. Examples shown in C major at 110 bpm.
  

  
    By genre
    By instrument
  

  
  

  
  

    
      Jazz
      

        
          
            Jazz Charleston
            charleston
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Chord on beat 1 and the and-of-2, leaving beats 3 and 4 open. Named for the 1920s dance; the defining syncopation of early jazz and Swing Era big-band writing.
          
            Jazz
            Swing
            Piano
            Full band
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Jazz waltz
            jazzwaltz
            3/4
            ▶
          
          
          Swung bass on beat 1, ride-cymbal implied chord on beat 3. The feel of bebop and post-bop piano trios — Evans, Peterson, Monk in 3/4.
          
            Jazz
            Bebop
            Piano trio
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

      
    

    
      Latin
      

        
          
            Bossa nova
            bossa
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Tresillo-derived bass pattern with chords on the off-beats; ride cymbal and side-stick percussion. Rooted in the guitar style of João Gilberto and the samba-jazz fusion of late-1950s Rio.
          
            Latin
            Jazz
            Brazilian
            Guitar
            Piano
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Tango
            tango
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Habanera rhythmic cell in the bass (long–short–short–long) with syncopated chords. Descended from Cuban contradanza via the Argentine arrabal — the tresillo pattern that underlies both the habanera and early tango.
          
            Latin
            Argentine
            Cuban
            Bandoneón
            Piano
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

      
    

    
      Waltz & Dance
      

        
          
            Waltz
            waltz
            3/4
            ▶
          
          
          Bass on beat 1, chord on beats 2 and 3. The universal dance-band formula for 3/4 time. The slight delay before the chord creates the characteristic lilt that distinguishes waltz from a march.
          
            Dance
            Classical
            Piano
            Orchestra
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Viennese waltz
            viennese
            3/4
            ▶
          
          
          A short downbeat chord followed by slightly-early beats 2 and 3 (the "anticipated" Viennese lilt). The elastic timing distinguishes the Strauss concert hall waltz from the plainer ballroom waltz.
          
            Dance
            Classical
            Romantic
            Orchestra
            Piano
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Boom-chick
            oompah
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Root on the downbeat, chord on every other beat. The engine of polka, march, and German dance-band styles; derived from tuba–horn interplay in brass ensembles.
          
            Folk
            Polka
            March
            Brass
            Accordion
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

      
    

    
      Folk & Country
      

        
          
            Folk — alternating bass
            folk
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Alternating root–fifth bass note with a chord on the off-beat. The defining guitar and banjo accompaniment pattern in American folk, bluegrass, and country; also called the "Carter scratch."
          
            Folk
            Country
            Bluegrass
            Guitar
            Banjo
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

      
    

    
      Pop & Rock
      

        
          
            Pop / ballad
            pop
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Bass on beats 1 and 3, steady quarter-note chords on all beats. The most common piano and keyboard pattern in popular music since the 1950s.
          
            Pop
            Rock
            Piano
            Keyboard
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Driving 8ths
            driving8
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Steady eighth-note chords with no gaps. Propulsive and energetic; fills every subdivision. Found across rock, country, and pop guitar — sometimes called a "strum pattern."
          
            Rock
            Country
            Pop
            Guitar
            Piano
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Offbeat chords
            offbeat
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Chord only on the off-beats, silence on the downbeats. The defining rhythmic gesture of ska and reggae; known as the "skank" or "chop." In reggae, the kick lands on beat 3 (the "one-drop").
          
            Ska
            Reggae
            Guitar
            Keyboard
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

      
    

    
      Classical & Piano
      

        
          
            Block chords
            block
            any
            ▶
          
          
          All voices together on every beat, sustained until the next chord. The simplest possible accompaniment; used for chorale writing, teaching chord shapes, and very slow tempos.
          
            Classical
            Chorale
            Piano
            Keyboard
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Piano arpeggio ↑
            arpflow
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Rising arpeggios through the full bar (root–third–fifth–octave). Characteristic of Romantic-era piano writing and nocturne accompaniment; adds forward motion when chord changes are sparse.
          
            Classical
            Romantic
            Piano
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Arpeggio (root–5th)
            arpcell
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Root and fifth as individual tones in a repeating per-beat cell. Creates a light, open texture that suggests harmonic motion without dense block chords; useful for harp or guitar figuration.
          
            Classical
            Pop
            Harp
            Guitar
            Piano
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

        
          
            Bass line (roots)
            bassroot
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Root note only in the bass, no chord voices above. Use as a harmonic foundation when the melody instrument carries the full harmonic content, or when learning to hear bass motion in isolation.
          
            Teaching
            Minimal
            Bass
          
          
            HistoryComing soon
            ListeningComing soon
          
        

      
    

  

  
  

    
      Piano & Keyboard
      
        
          Block chordsblockany
          Sustained block voicing on every beat. The starting point for learning chord shapes and for chorale-style writing.
          ClassicalChorale
        
        
          Waltzwaltz3/4
          Left-hand bass on 1, right-hand chord on 2–3. The standard piano waltz texture from Chopin through the ballroom repertoire.
          DanceClassical
        
        
          Pop / balladpopany
          Bass on 1 and 3, chords on every beat. Ubiquitous in pop piano; easy to adapt to either hands-together or stride-style playing.
          PopRock
        
        
          Piano arpeggio ↑arpflow4/4
          Rising broken chord through the full bar. Nocturne and ballad texture; standard left-hand figure in Romantic piano writing.
          ClassicalRomantic
        
        
          Viennese waltzviennese3/4
          Elastic 3/4 with the characteristic early-beat anticipation. Strauss orchestra and salon piano repertoire.
          DanceClassical
        
        
          Jazz Charlestoncharleston4/4
          Syncopated 4/4 comping with a chord on beat 1 and the and-of-2. Common in Stride, Swing, and mainstream jazz piano.
          JazzSwing
        
        
          Jazz waltzjazzwaltz3/4
          Swung bass on 1, light chord on 3. The texture of jazz piano trios playing in 3 — Evans, Peterson, Monk.
          JazzBebop
        
      
    

    
      Guitar & Plucked Strings
      
        
          Folk — alternating bassfolkany
          Thumb alternates root–fifth in the bass; fingers strum the chord on the off-beat. The foundational fingerpicking and flatpicking pattern in folk, bluegrass, and country guitar.
          FolkCountryBluegrass
        
        
          Driving 8thsdriving8any
          Steady downstroke eighth notes, no rests. The strum pattern behind countless rock, country, and pop songs.
          RockCountryPop
        
        
          Offbeat chordsoffbeatany
          The ska/reggae "skank": chord only between the beats. In reggae the downstroke is muted, leaving only the upstroke chop.
          SkaReggae
        
        
          Bossa novabossa4/4
          Tresillo bass with off-beat chords. João Gilberto's original bossa guitar part is the blueprint; the pattern is also widely adapted to keyboard.
          LatinBrazilianJazz
        
        
          Arpeggio (root–5th)arpcellany
          Open-voiced ascending tones per beat. Evokes harp, classical guitar, and lute figuration; widely used in Renaissance and Baroque lute tablature.
          ClassicalPop
        
      
    

    
      Brass & Ensemble
      
        
          Boom-chickoompahany
          Tuba carries the root on 1; horns answer with the chord on 2 (and 4). The pattern maps directly to the brass section interplay in polka bands, march bands, and German Blasmusik.
          PolkaMarchFolk
        
        
          Tangotango4/4
          Habanera cell in the bass with syncopated upper chords. In Argentine tango this interplay moves between bandoneón and piano; in orchestral tango (Piazzolla) it drives the entire ensemble.
          LatinArgentine
        
      
    

    
      Bass & Foundation
      
        
          Bass line (roots)bassrootany
          Root note only, no chords. Use this when practicing bass lines in isolation, or when the melody is dense enough to imply the harmony on its own.
          TeachingMinimal
